mnemonics: My Very Eager Mother Just Served Us Nachos (for auction types: My = Myerson, Very = Vickrey, Eager = English, Mother = Myerson, Just = Japanese, Served = Second-Price, Us = Uniform Price, Nachos = Name Your Price)
keyPoints: The Revenue Equivalence Theorem states that under certain conditions, all standard auction formats yield the same expected revenue to the seller.
A Vickrey (second-price) auction is strategy-proof; a bidder's dominant strategy is to bid their true valuation.
The Winner's Curse describes the phenomenon where the winner of a common-value auction tends to overpay because their winning bid was based on an overestimation of the item's value.
commonMistakes: Assuming all auction types (e.g., first-price vs. second-price) result in the same revenue for the seller, ignoring the assumptions of the Revenue Equivalence Theorem.
Bidding one's true valuation in a first-price sealed-bid auction, which is not a dominant strategy (this is only optimal in a second-price auction).
Failing to account for the Winner's Curse in common-value auctions and thus bidding too aggressively without shading the bid.
